rxn_class_summary: Mn-catalyzed electrochemical 1,2-diazidation of alkenes using NaN3 as the azide source under anodic oxidation conditions
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation regenerates [Mn(III)]–N3 from [Mn(II)]; Mn mediator lowers the potential required to oxidize N3- to an azidyl radical equivalent that is delivered to the alkene

key_intermediates.name: beta-azido carbon-centered radical (from azidyl addition to alkene)
[Mn(II)] reduced manganese species (resting after azidyl transfer)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes [Mn(II)] to [Mn(III)]-N3, which delivers an azidyl radical to the alkene generating a C-centered radical; a second [Mn(III)]-N3 (regenerated by anodic oxidation with N3-) transfers a second azide to the carbon radical to give the 1,2-diazide.

probe_reagents.name: cell potential variation (Ecell 1.9-2.3 V)
probe_reagents.observation: Lowering Ecell from 2.3 V to 1.9 V with 10 mol% MnBr2·4H2O improves selectivity (16:17 from 3:1 to 7:1) and isolated yield (from <10% to 60%), consistent with controlled anodic oxidation of Mn/N3-

notes: Mechanism is proposed (Fig. 2d) without explicit radical traps, KIE, EPR, or DFT shown on these pages. A major byproduct (17, a triazide) suggests overoxidation/competing radical pathways at higher potentials. The Mn catalyst plays a dual role: lowering the oxidation potential of N3- and chemoselectively delivering azidyl first to the alkene and then to the C-radical, accounting for tolerance of oxidatively sensitive FGs (alcohols, aldehydes, sulfides, amines, ferrocenes).
